carrier_arguments: This estimate is priced based on estimated market pricing for the cost of materials, labor, and other factors at the time of the loss.

Adjustments in market pricing and timing of the repairs may impact the final cost of covered repairs. Should you or your contractor find during the course of repairs that the actual and necessary cost of covered repairs at the time of repair is higher than this one, you should contact us prior to beginning repairs. State Farm will work with you and your contractor to determine the actual and necessary cost of covered repairs at the time repairs will be completed, subject to policy terms, conditions and limits.
We want you to receive quality repair work to restore the damages to your property.
State Farm is a Participating Insurer of the California Earthquake Authority, P.O. Box 52295, Phoenix, AZ 85072-2295, CEA Fax: (886) 529-8467
carrier.name: State Farm

summary_paragraphs: Please find enclosed the complete forensic documentation package for the property at 9130 Avon Lake Rd, Lodi, OH 44254, USA. Our independent inspection has documented storm-related damage to the Roofing.

The enclosed reports include annotated photography of all identified damage, an Xactimate-format replacement cost estimate, and a forensic causation analysis establishing the relationship between documented weather events and observed damage.
Please do not hesitate to contact us should you require any additional information or wish to schedule a joint inspection.

code_violations.requirement: Asphalt shingles shall be installed in accordance with this section AND the manufacturer's installation instructions — adopts manufacturer spec as code-enforceable requirement
code_violations.status: Required — manufacturer spec controls fastener count, exposure, starter, drip edge, and warranty-critical details

code_violations.requirement: Drip edge required at eaves and rake edges of shingle roofs; min 2-inch overlap at joints, min 1/4-inch projection below sheathing
Underlayment shall be applied in accordance with manufacturer's installation instructions — ASTM D226 / D4869 / D6757 labeling required
Factory starter strip course required along eaves and rakes with pre-applied sealant strip at roof edge; field-cut shingles do not qualify as starter (no sealant = wind-uplift failure)
code_violations.status: Required — included in scope per manufacturer wind-warranty spec

code_violations.requirement: Ice barrier required in areas where the average daily temperature in January is ≤25°F or where ice damming has a history; min 2 feet inside exterior wall line (Climate Zones 5A+ including OH/NY)
Flashings shall be installed at wall and roof intersections, wherever there is a change in roof slope or direction, and around roof openings

conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 18 documented findings, the property at 9130 Avon Lake Rd, Lodi, OH 44254, USA sustained confirmed wind storm damage from the March 13, 2026 event. The weight of evidence is substantial: widespread shingle loss across the rear elevation exposing both synthetic underlayment and bare deck, wind-creased tabs with sharp horizontal fold lines at the nail line exhibiting clean mechanical seal breaks, and an exposed roofing nail with zero rust formation — forensic confirmation that the damage occurred during the recent storm rather than representing a pre-existing condition. The directional damage pattern, concentrated on the rear windward elevation while the front elevation remains largely intact, is consistent with a single directional wind event and eliminates age-related deterioration as a competing cause.

The confirmed wind damage to the Architectural Laminated Comp Shingle roofing system and associated components on the rear elevation requires full slope replacement rather than spot repair. The existing older 3-tab shingle product features a narrow 5-inch exposure that cannot be matched with any currently manufactured shingle product; current metric 3-tab shingles carry a 5-5/8-inch exposure, which would create cumulative course misalignment across the slope, resulting in an aesthetically inconsistent and functionally compromised repair. Furthermore, the widespread areas of exposed deck and underlayment constitute a violation of IRC R905.2.6 as adopted under the RCO, which mandates a complete weather-resistant roof covering — partial patching with mismatched materials cannot restore code compliance.

Based on the documented damage, applicable building codes under the RCO, and recognized industry standards, our analysis identifies the need for full replacement of the rear roof slope, including removal of all remaining compromised shingles, inspection and repair of any damaged deck sheathing, and installation of a new code-compliant roofing system with proper underlayment, flashing, and ridge components. The forensic evidence further supports inclusion of the rear elevation — which sustained the most severe and widespread wind damage — in any claim scope, as the directional damage pattern, mechanical seal failures, and material discontinuation collectively render spot repair infeasible and non-code-compliant.

This determination is supported by HAAG Engineering standards for wind damage assessment, which classify wind-creased tabs with mechanical seal breaks at the bond line as functional damage requiring replacement rather than cosmetic repair. NRCA guidelines further establish that mixing discontinued shingle products with current-production materials compromises weatherproofing performance and manufacturer warranty coverage. All findings documented in this report are grounded in physical forensic evidence observed and photographed at the property, interpreted in accordance with these recognized professional standards and the IRC as adopted under Ohio's Residential Code (RCO).
forensic_findings.damage_summary: Rear elevation of this 3-tab roof sustained significant wind damage during the March 13, 2026 storm event. Multiple shingles are missing across the rear slope, exposing underlayment and bare deck. Remaining shingles show wind creases at the nail line with clean mechanical seal breaks confirming forcible wind separation — not age-related adhesive failure. An exposed nail with no rust confirms recent storm timing. A directional damage pattern is evident: damage is concentrated on the rear elevation (windward face) while the front face appears largely intact, consistent with the contractor's observation that State Farm paid for the front but the rear — which sustained the worst damage — was not included. The older 3-tab shingles with their narrow exposure cannot be matched with any current product, making spot repair infeasible and full replacement necessary. Photos 6-10 document wind damage to the rear elevation of an older 3-tab asphalt shingle roof. Close-up inspection reveals wind-creased tabs with sharp horizontal fold lines at the nail line, mechanically broken seal strips with clean separation, and a lifted/turned-back shingle tab with a pry bar demonstrating zero adhesion. The final two photos show a large area of shingles stripped to expose RhinoRoof U20 synthetic underlayment — consistent with widespread wind removal on the rear slope. The contractor notes damage is widespread only on the rear elevation, confirming a directional wind pattern (rear = windward face). The 3-tab shingles appear to be an older discontinued product; spot repair with current metric shingles would create exposure misalignment, supporting full slope replacement. The combination of creased tabs, broken seals, and missing shingles across the rear elevation constitutes functional damage from the March 13, 2026 wind event. Photo 11 shows the rear elevation of the shop roof with RhinoRoof U20 synthetic underlayment exposed where shingles have been removed or blown off. A tape measure documents the extent of exposed underlayment. Per contractor notes, widespread wind damage exists on the rear elevation of this older 3-tab roof. The exposed underlayment with no shingles present confirms catastrophic wind loss on this slope from the March 13, 2026 storm event.

executive_summary: On March 13, 2026, the property at 9130 Avon Lake Rd, Lodi, OH 44254, USA sustained storm damage from a documented wind event. The subject property features an architectural laminated comp shingle system on the main dwelling and an older 3-tab asphalt shingle system on the shop structure. An 11-photograph forensic inspection was conducted to document the type, extent, and causation of wind damage across all roof elevations, with particular focus on the rear slopes of both structures where damage concentration was most severe.

The rear elevations of both the dwelling and the shop exhibit widespread wind damage characterized by missing shingles, exposed RhinoRoof U20 synthetic underlayment, and bare roof deck. Close-range inspection of remaining shingles on the rear slope reveals sharp horizontal wind creases at the nail line with mechanically broken seal strips exhibiting clean separation — forensic indicators consistent with forcible wind uplift rather than age-related adhesive degradation. An exposed fastener with no oxidation corroborates recent storm-period timing, and the directional concentration of damage on the rear (windward) elevation, with the front elevation remaining largely intact, confirms a coherent wind-load pattern consistent with the March 13, 2026 event.

The damaged 3-tab shingles are an older discontinued product with a nominal exposure dimension that cannot be matched by any currently manufactured metric shingle; spot repairs would produce visible exposure misalignment and fail to restore a uniform weather-resistant surface, rendering piecemeal repair technically infeasible. Under the Residential Code of Ohio (RCO), referencing IRC Section R905.2, replacement roofing materials must provide equivalent weather protection, and mixed installations of mismatched shingle profiles compromise both aesthetic uniformity and functional water-shedding performance. Full slope replacement of the rear elevations on both the dwelling and shop structures is therefore the minimum scope necessary to restore code-compliant roof system integrity following the March 13, 2026 wind event.
key_arguments: Widespread missing shingles on rear elevation with exposed underlayment and bare deck — emergency tarp in place confirms active leak vulnerability.
Exposed roofing nail at shingle joint shows zero rust — forensic proof that shingle loss occurred during the recent March 13, 2026 wind event, not a pre-existing condition.
Wind-creased tab with clean mechanical seal break at bond line confirms functional damage per HAAG standards — replacement required.
Directional damage pattern: rear (windward) elevation shows severe wind damage while front elevation is largely intact, consistent with a single directional wind event.
Older 3-tab shingle product with narrow exposure cannot be matched with any current shingle product on the market — spot repair is infeasible, full slope replacement required.
Contractor notes State Farm paid for front face but rear elevation sustained the most severe damage and was not included in the original settlement — supplemental claim warranted.
Wind-creased tabs with sharp horizontal fold lines at the nail line — functional damage per HAAG standard requiring replacement.
Mechanically broken seal strips with clean separation confirm wind-driven failure, not age-related adhesive degradation.
Pry bar test demonstrates zero remaining seal adhesion on affected shingles — tabs lift freely without resistance.
Widespread shingle loss on rear elevation exposes synthetic underlayment across a large area — directional pattern confirms windward face damage from March 13, 2026 event.
Older 3-tab product is likely discontinued; current metric 3-tab shingles have 5-5/8" exposure, creating cumulative misalignment if spot-repaired on a 5" exposure roof.
Damage concentrated on rear elevation with front reportedly intact — directional wind pattern is forensic proof of a single wind event.
Rear shop elevation shows complete shingle loss exposing RhinoRoof U20 synthetic underlayment — consistent with catastrophic wind stripping from March 13, 2026 storm
Tape measure documents width of exposed area confirming widespread damage on rear slope as noted by contractor
Green cap nails visible through underlayment indicate recent underlayment installation as temporary weather protection after wind loss event
Contractor confirms SF has paid for front face but rear elevation damage remains unaddressed — directional wind pattern (rear > front) is classic windward-face concentration
Older 3-tab shingles are more vulnerable to wind uplift due to age-degraded seal strips — storm event exploited this vulnerability on the windward rear slope
Estimated roof age: ~12 years (installed circa 2014) — Shingle Type: Laminate/Architectural shingle

code_citation.requirement: Existing roofing materials shall be removed before applying new roofing when existing roof has two or more applications of any type of roof covering
code_citation.supplement_argument: Complete tear-off is required per code for a code-compliant installation. Overlay is not code-compliant when damage necessitates deck inspection.

code_citation.requirement: Roofing shall be applied in accordance with manufacturer's installation instructions. Manufacturer instructions carry the force of building code.
code_citation.supplement_argument: Per R905.1, manufacturer installation instructions are legally binding building code. All components specified by the manufacturer (starter, underlayment, flashing) are code-required, not optional upgrades.
code_citation.title: Manufacturer Installation Instructions = Law

photo_annotations.p03_01: RhinoRoof underlayment tarp secured over missing shingles on rear slope — active wind damage area.
photo_annotations.p03_02: Exposed nail at shingle joint with no rust confirms recent wind-driven shingle loss from March 2026 event.
photo_annotations.p03_03: Wind-creased tab reveals clean mechanical seal break at bond line — functional damage confirmed.
photo_annotations.p04_01: Displaced shingle tab at flashing transition exposes gap — wind-driven separation at vulnerable joint.
photo_annotations.p04_02: Tarp pulled back reveals widespread missing 3-tab shingles on rear slope — exposed underlayment and deck.
photo_annotations.p04_03: Wind-creased 3-tab shingle with sharp fold at nail line; broken seal strip confirms functional wind damage.
photo_annotations.p05_01: Close-up of mechanically broken seal strip at shingle overlap — clean separation consistent with wind event.
photo_annotations.p05_02: Pry bar lifts shingle tab freely — zero seal adhesion confirms wind-broken bond, not age degradation.
photo_annotations.p05_03: Large section of shingles removed exposing RhinoRoof U20 underlayment — widespread wind loss on rear slope.
photo_annotations.p06_01: Wider view of exposed RhinoRoof U20 synthetic underlayment after wind-stripped shingles on rear elevation.
